label: Heel rubbing blister without diabetic-foot or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated heel rubbing blister.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated heel rubbing blister. We did not find infection, burn injury, widespread blistering, diabetic-foot risk, or another emergency problem today.
home: Protect the blister from more rubbing or pressure.
Keep the area clean and dry unless your clinician told you something different.
Do not pop, cut, or peel the blister unless your clinician instructed you to.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use creams, ointments, or antibiotics only if your clinician prescribed or recommended them.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, fever, or worsening swelling.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, widespread blisters, severe pain, or a blister from a burn or chemical exposure.
Numbness, black skin, poor circulation, a new foot ulcer, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, podiatry, urgent care, or the ED if the blister is not healing or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated heel rubbing blister.

No infection, burn pathway, widespread blistering, medication reaction, mucosal involvement, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, pus, open infected wound, fever, systemic illness, burn, chemical/electrical injury, widespread blistering, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction concern, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, diabetic foot risk, neuropathy, PVD, or specialist-directed wound/podiatry plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports blister framing, protection from rubbing, simple skin care, and escalation for infection, burns, widespread blistering, mucosal lesions, or severe skin reactions. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated heel rubbing blister with infection, burn, high-risk skin, and diabetic-foot red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated heel rubbing blister
no infection
no burn or severe skin reaction
no diabetic-foot pathway

label: Localized glove contact rash without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from glove contact.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized glove contact skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, skin infection, or another rash emergency today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, or mouth sores.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized glove contact skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin infection, rapidly progressive rash, burn or chemical exposure, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, ocular, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized glove contact skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, mucosal, and severe-rash red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized glove contact skin reaction
localized reaction only
no anaphylaxis
no infection, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags
